# AIDDISON™ AI Drug Discovery

## BREAKTHROUGHS IN DRUG DISCOVERY BEGIN HERE

AIDDISON™ software empowers medicinal chemists by streamlining the entire drug discovery process. Leveraging generative AI and advanced CADD methods, AIDDISON™ integrates ligand-based and structure-based approaches into a single, easy-to-use platform. From hit identification to lead optimization, it provides all the tools needed to move from idea to actionable insight with greater efficiency and confidence.

![A scientist in a lab coat is seated at a desk, interacting with a computer screen displaying a molecular structure. ## Related Product Resources

By seamlessly integrating generative models, ADMET property predictions, searches in vast chemical spaces, and molecular docking, AIDDISON™ provides a sophisticated platform for modern drug discovery.

AIDDISON™ uses a scientific approach to first generate thousands of viable candidate molecules as the starting point in an analysis.

Machine learning, deep learning, and generative AI can help scientists generate structures for new drug candidates and explore protein-ligand binding before starting any experiments in a wet lab.

This playbook provides a comprehensive guide to integrating AI into existing drug discovery programs.

As our access to more diverse chemical space expands, fueled by advances in combinatorial chemistry, navigating this space becomes ever more sophisticated and essential for drug discovery.

Our AI-powered drug discovery platform will help drug designers explore vast chemical space and design successful drug candidates in minutes.

AIDDISON™ software-as-a-service platform bridges the gap between virtual molecule design and real-world manufacturing.

Before the new AI-powered drug discovery platform hit the market, it took trials, tribulations and a napkin.

AIDDISON™ combines the power of artificial intelligence (AI) and computer-aided drug design (CADD) tools into a single integrated platform for virtual screening, scaffold hopping, hit identification, and lead optimization in medicinal chemistry.

--- SOURCE 3: Generative AI for drug discovery and protein design ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107

SUMMARY:
Generative AI for drug discovery and protein design: the next frontier in AI-driven molecular science - ScienceDirect Generative AI for drug discovery and protein design: the next frontier in AI-driven molecular science This review systematically delineates the theoretical underpinnings, algorithmic architectures, and translational applications of deep generative models—including variational autoencoders (VAEs), generative adversarial networks (GANs), autoregressive transformers, and score-based denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs)—in the rational design of bioactive small molecules and functional proteins. Furthermore, we survey advancements in graph-based molecular generative frameworks, LLM-guided protein sequence modeling, and diffusion-based structural prediction pipelines (e.g., RFdiffusion, FrameDiff), which have demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in de novo protein engineering and conformational sampling. Generative AI is also catalyzing a paradigm shift in structure-based drug discovery via AI-augmented molecular docking (e.g., DiffDock), end-to-end binding affinity prediction, and quantum chemistry-informed neural potentials.

FULL CONTENT:
Generative AI for drug discovery and protein design: the next frontier in AI-driven molecular science - ScienceDirect

Review Article

Generative AI for drug discovery and protein design: the next frontier in AI-driven molecular science
=====================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Uddalak Das

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.medidd.2025.100213](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.medidd.2025.100213 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2590098625000107&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a disruptive paradigm in molecular science, enabling algorithmic navigation and construction of chemical and proteomic spaces through data-driven modeling. This review systematically delineates the theoretical underpinnings, algorithmic architectures, and translational applications of deep generative models—including variational autoencoders (VAEs), generative adversarial networks (GANs), autoregressive transformers, and score-based denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs)—in the rational design of bioactive small molecules and functional proteins. We examine the role of latent space learning, probabilistic manifold exploration, and reinforcement learning in inverse molecular design, focusing on optimization of pharmacologically relevant objectives such as ADMET profiles, synthetic accessibility, and target affinity. Furthermore, we survey advancements in graph-based molecular generative frameworks, LLM-guided protein sequence modeling, and diffusion-based structural prediction pipelines (e.g., RFdiffusion, FrameDiff), which have demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in de novo protein engineering and conformational sampling. Generative AI is also catalyzing a paradigm shift in structure-based drug discovery via AI-augmented molecular docking (e.g., DiffDock), end-to-end binding affinity prediction, and quantum chemistry-informed neural potentials. We explore the convergence of generative models with Bayesian retrosynthesis planners, self-supervised pretraining on ultra-large chemical corpora, and multimodal integration of omics-derived features for precision therapeutics. Finally, we discuss translational milestones wherein AI-designed ligands and proteins have progressed to preclinical and clinical validation, and speculate on the synthesis of generative AI, closed-loop automation, and quantum computing in future autonomous molecular design ecosystems.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000119)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000120)

Keywords
--------

Generative AI

Molecular design

Protein engineering

Diffusion models

Drug discovery

1. Introduction
---------------

Drug discovery is traditionally costly, slow, and failure-prone [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0005)]. Preclinical discovery takes over five years, consuming one-third of total costs [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0010)]. With fewer than 10% of candidates succeeding, R&D expenditure per new drug exceeds $2 billion, mainly due to failures [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0015),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0020)]. Many failures stem from safety/efficacy issues emerging late [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0025)]. AI has recently accelerated _in silico_ modeling across the pipeline, improving QSAR-based virtual screening and ML-driven protein engineering [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0030)].

Historically, rule-based de novo drug design (e.g., LUDI, PRO_LIGAND) explored limited chemical space due to human bias [[[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0030), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0035), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0040)]. Generative AI overcomes this by learning molecular patterns and creating novel compounds [[[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0045)][10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0050)]. Unlike classical methods that recombine known motifs, it explores uncharted chemical space [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0055)]. Given an estimated>10 60 drug-like molecules, AI efficiently samples viable candidates via chemical manifolds [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0060)]. AI designs millions of molecules in the time it takes for manual design, optimizing multiple properties simultaneously [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0065)]. Recent pipelines enhance synthetic feasibility and drug-likeness.

AI-driven discovery integrates generative models with computational chemistry, transitioning from empirical screening to rational design [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0070)]. Mid-20th-century drug discovery relied on trial-and-error; later, structure-based (X-ray) and ligand-based (pharmacophore) methods emerged [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0075)]. The 2000 s saw docking and QSAR improvements [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0080)]. AI now creates novel molecules, with the first AI-designed drug (DSP-1181) entering trials in 2020 [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0085)].

In AI workflows, generative models design de novo molecules, filtered via predictive models (binding affinity, ADMET) [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0090),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0095)]. Top hits undergo docking, synthesis planning, and wet-lab validation. Retrosynthesis AI suggests lab synthesis routes, while experimental feedback refines models [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0100)]. AI continuously self-improves, navigating chemical space intelligently. Future directions include autonomous discovery, quantum computing, and regulatory frameworks [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0105)].

2. Deep generative models: core architectures
---------------------------------------------

Deep generative models enable de novo molecular design by learning statistical patterns in chemical datasets to generate novel, valid compounds. The five principal architectures and optimization strategies are illustrated in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#f0005), providing a conceptual overview of their roles in molecular generation. A comparative overview of key deep generative model types—including VAEs, GANs, transformer-based architectures, and denoising diffusion models—is presented in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#t0005), highlighting their core principles, representational strategies, and benchmarking performance across molecular datasets.

![Image 11](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590098625000107-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (272KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590098625000107-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (272KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590098625000107-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Architectures of deep generative models and latent space optimization in molecular design. **(A)** Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), **(B)** Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), **(C)** Transformer-based models, and **(D)** Denoising Diffusion Models generate molecules using distinct mechanisms. **(E)** Latent space optimization explores continuous chemical manifolds to design molecules with desired properties.

Table 1. Comparative Overview of Core Deep Generative Model Architectures in Molecular Design.

| Model Type | Key Principle | Strengths | Weaknesses / Limitations | Common Molecular Representation(s) | Primary Application Focus | Notable Examples / Tools | Performance Metrics | Benchmark Dataset | Reference |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) | Encode molecules to a latent space and decode to reconstruct them, optimizing ELBO. | Smooth latent space; interpolatable; stable training; grounded in Bayesian inference. | Can generate invalid molecules; latent space may not align with properties directly. | SMILES, Molecular Graphs, 3D Coordinates | Small Molecules | Junction Tree VAE, Grammar VAE, G-SchNet | Validity: 60–98%; Uniqueness: High; SA Score: Moderate | ZINC, QM9 | [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0110)] |
| Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) | Generator–discriminator adversarial training to mimic data distribution. | Encourages realistic outputs; supports property conditioning; reinforcement learning compatible. | Training instability; mode collapse; discrete molecule generation is non-differentiable. | SMILES, Molecular Graphs | Small Molecules | ORGAN, MolGAN, Adversarial Threshold Neural Computer | Validity: ∼100% (MolGAN); SA: High; Diversity: Moderate | QM9, ZINC | [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0115)] |
| Transformer-based Models | Self-attention to learn dependencies across sequences. | Captures long-range dependencies; language-model pretraining; excellent sequence modeling. | Requires large data; training is resource-intensive; property control may be indirect. | SMILES, SELFIES, Protein Sequences | Both | ChemBERTa, ProGen, GPT-chem, TAPE | Perplexity: Low; Validity: ∼99%; Sequence Identity (Proteins): 20–40% | Uniprot, ZINC, PDB | [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0120)] |
| Denoising Diffusion Models (DDPMs) | Learn to reverse noise corruption to generate data from random noise. | High sample diversity; theoretical completeness; works in molecular and 3D space. | Slow generation; computationally expensive; still maturing in chemistry. | Graphs, 3D Coordinates, Atom Matrices | Both | GeoDiff, RFdiffusion, DiffDock | RMSD (DiffDock): ∼1.5 Å; Validity: ∼100%; Success Rate: >75% | PDBbind, PoseBusters | [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0125)] |

### 2.1. Variational autoencoders (VAEs)

A VAE comprises an encoder that compresses a molecule (typically represented as a SMILES string or molecular graph) into a continuous latent vector, and a decoder that reconstructs the molecule from this vector [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0130)]. Trained on large compound datasets, the encoder maps structurally similar molecules to proximate points in latent space, effectively learning a continuous chemical manifold [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0135)]. The training objective is to maximize data reconstruction likelihood while enforcing the latent vectors to follow a smooth, multi-dimensional Gaussian distribution using Kullback–Leibler (KL) regularization, enabling meaningful interpolation [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0140)]. The model optimizes the evidence lower bound (ELBO):L VAE=E q∅(z x)ln p θ x z-D KL(q∅(z x)‖p z)where q∅(z x) is the encoder (approximate posterior), and p θ x z is the decoder (likelihood). Once trained, a random vector z p(z) can be decoded into a novel molecule. Demonstrated for drug-like molecules by Gómez-Bombarelli _et al_. (2016–2017), VAEs enabled direct optimization in latent space to search for molecules with improved properties [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0145)]. The continuous latent space allows smooth interpolation between compounds (chemical morphing) to explore analogs [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0150)]. However, early SMILES-based VAEs often generated invalid or implausible structures [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0155)]. To address this, chemically informed decoders like Junction-Tree VAE were introduced, generating molecules as a tree of substructures, ensuring valency constraints are satisfied [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0160)]. VAEs have also been extended to 3D molecular conformations [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0135)].

VAEs provide a principled framework grounded in Bayesian inference, ensuring training stability and interpretability [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0165)]. Despite limitations in output validity and reconstruction bias, VAEs are frequently combined with latent space optimization techniques such as Bayesian optimization or gradient-based methods to identify latent vectors yielding molecules with desirable properties [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0170)]. While their abstract latent space can hinder direct property optimization, VAEs remain foundational in molecular generation [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0175)].

### 2.2. Generative adversarial networks (GANs)

GANs approach generation differently. Instead of modeling data likelihood explicitly, GANs set up a two-player game between a generator and a discriminator [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0180)]. The generator creates molecules from random noise, while the discriminator distinguishes real from generated samples. Adversarial training forces the generator to produce realistic molecules [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0185)]. Applied to molecules in 2018 (e.g., ORGAN by Insilico Medicine), GANs used an RNN generator to output SMILES strings and a discriminator that rewarded drug-like outputs [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0115)]. Conditional GANs guide generation towards desired properties (e.g., target binding) by conditioning on context [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0190)].

GANs face challenges in molecular domains due to discrete outputs: the generator’s character-sequence output is non-differentiable. Solutions include policy gradient reinforcement learning and differentiable relaxations. Insilico’s Adversarial Threshold Neural Computer integrated GANs with reinforcement learning, using a differentiable neural computer as the generator and providing external rewards based on pharmacological properties [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0195)]. This hybrid generated a high percentage of valid, unique, and property-optimized molecules, while also incorporating synthesizability constraints [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0200)]. MolGAN, another milestone, generated molecular graphs (atom and bond matrices) directly. On the QM9 dataset, it achieved nearly 100% validity, improved synthetic accessibility, and solubility profiles compared to ORGAN [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0205)].

Despite these advances, GANs may suffer from mode collapse and training instability. Their learned distribution might not cover the full chemical space [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0210)]. However, conditional GANs remain powerful for generating analogs of lead compounds [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0215)]. Overall, GANs introduce adversarial learning into molecular design, emphasizing realistic outputs and targeted objectives, though maintaining output diversity and validity requires care.

### 2.3. Transformer-based models

Transformer-based models, originally developed for NLP, are also applied to molecular science [[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0220)]. Their self-attention mechanism captures long-range dependencies in sequences. For small molecules (SMILES or SELFIES) and protein sequences, transformers treat chemistry as a language. For example, ChemBERTa, trained on millions of SMILES strings using masked token prediction, produces rich, label-free molecular embeddings suitable for downstream tasks such as property prediction or generative modelling [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0120),[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0225)].

Transformers can generate molecules token-by-token (GPT-style), learning chemical syntax analogous to grammar. Such models can be conditioned to bias outputs toward specific property profiles [[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0230)]. In lead optimization, transformers have proposed structural analogs based on known series [[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0235)]. Protein language models (e.g., ProGen with 1.2B parameters trained on∼280 M protein sequences) treat amino acid sequences like sentences. ProGen has generated functional enzymes with catalytic activity comparable to natural lysozymes, despite∼30% sequence identity. X-ray crystallography confirmed correct folds and active-site geometries [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0240),[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0245)]. Transformers also support sequence-to-sequence tasks like codon optimization or property prediction [[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0250)].

These models bridge sequence and structure, enabling protein and molecule generation via attention-based encoding of complex dependencies. They leverage large unlabeled datasets for self-supervised learning, yielding representations useful for property prediction, structure generation, and analog design [[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0255),[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0260)].

### 2.4. Denoising diffusion models (DDPMs)

DDPMs represent the latest generative modeling wave. They iteratively corrupt data with Gaussian noise and learn to reverse this process. A forward Markov chain adds noise over _T_ steps until the sample becomes pure noise. A neural network then learns to reverse the corruption by predicting denoised data at each step. Training minimizes a reweighted variational bound, typically reducing to the loss between predicted and true noise, equivalent to learning the score function ∇log p(x t) [[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0265)].

The training objective of DDPMs is derived from a variational lower bound on the negative log-likelihood of the data. Let _x_ 0 ∼ _q_(_x_ 0) be the data distribution. A forward diffusion process gradually adds Gaussian noise [[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0270)]:q(x t|x t-1)=N(x t;1-((β t x t-1,β t I

The reverse process is parameterized as _p_ θ (_x t_−1|_x t_), typically modelled as a Gaussian with mean predicted by a neural network ∊θ((_x t_,_t_) [[55](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0275)]. The training objective becomes:L simple=E t,x 0,∊N(0,I)[∊-∊0 x t,t 2]

This is equivalent to denoising score matching: learning the gradient of the log-density ∇_xt_ log _p_(_x t_), i.e., the score function. The full derivation shows that this is a reweighted variational bound under the ELBO [[56](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0280)].

Generation begins from random noise and progressively reconstructs data, enabling generation in the original space (e.g., 3D coordinates of atoms) rather than latent space. This supports highly diverse and high-quality outputs [[57](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0285),[58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0290)]. Diffusion models have been applied to 2D molecular graphs, 3D conformations, and protein structures [[59](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0295)]. For instance, graph diffusion models like GeoDiff [[60](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0300)] and RFdiffusion [[61](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0305)] add noise to adjacency and node feature matrices or 3D coordinates and reconstruct valid molecular structures, preserving symmetry and chemical rules. DiffDock [[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0310)], using SE(3)-equivariant diffusion, generates ligand poses in binding sites by diffusing atomic positions.

DiffDock, trained on cross-docked PDBbind complexes, achieves a median RMSD of 1.5 Å for ligand heavy atoms and success rate>75% at RMSD<2.0 Å across diverse targets. It outperforms classical docking tools (e.g., AutoDock Vina, GNINA) in pose prediction accuracy, particularly in flexible protein pockets. In benchmarking, DiffDock was up to 40X faster at inference when using DDIM acceleration [[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0310)].

Mathematically, as T→∞, the model can approximate any data distribution, offering theoretical guarantees absent in VAEs or GANs [[63](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0315)]. Though generation is slow due to multiple neural evaluations, recent innovations like DDIMs have reduced required steps.

DDPMs are computationally intensive, typically requiring 1000–4000 iterative denoising steps per sample, which significantly burdens GPU resources and runtime. To mitigate this cost, several strategies have been developed. DDIMs employ a non-Markovian deterministic sampling procedure that enables high-quality sample generation with only∼50–250 steps—achieving up to a 20X reduction in sampling time without substantial degradation in fidelity [[64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590098625000107#b0320)]. Further acceleration is achieved via distilled diffusion models, such as SGMs, which distill long denoising trajectories into compact student models capable of generating outputs in just 1–2 function evaluations. Building on this, methods like ScoreNet and Progressive Distillation fu

Creative Biolabs has established the AI-assisted bioinformatics analysis platform to support our customers in molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulation,

FULL CONTENT:
![Dataverify](static/images/logo.png?v1.0.190828)

# AI assisted Molecular Docking Service

Molecular docking is a modeling technique that is used to predict how two or more molecular structures fit together. The protein or nucleic acid can form a supramolecular complex with small molecules, which plays a major role in the dynamics of the protein. Moreover, the ability to form complexes may enhance or inhibit the biological function of the protein. Molecular docking can describe the behavior of small molecules in the binding pockets of target proteins. The technique aims to determine the proper positions of ligands in the binding pocket of protein and to predict their affinity. Because of its relatively low-cost implications and apparent ease of use, molecular docking has emerged as a key technique in the drug development toolkit, enjoying rising popularity among academic groups. **Creative Biolabs** has established the AI-assisted bioinformatics analysis platform to support our customers in molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulation, virtual screening, and Quantitative structural activity relationship (QSAR) analysis.

![Schematic representation of molecular docking elements.](static/img/2-4-5-1-AI-assisted-Molecular-Docking-1.jpg)Fig.1 Elements in molecular docking.1

![Schematic representation of molecular docking elements.](static/img/2-4-5-1-AI-assisted-Molecular-Docking-1.jpg)

Based on the types of ligands, there are three molecular docking model as below.

The crucial process in enzymatic processes is ligand binding. Therefore, a rational approach to drug design may be based on a thorough understanding of interactions between small compounds and proteins. In order to create compounds that treat a wide variety of significant illnesses, such as malignancies or cardiovascular disorders, molecular docking was often taken into consideration. Finding the best binding between a small molecule (ligand) and a protein is the goal of protein-ligand docking. The purpose of protein-ligand docking is to find the optimal binding between a small molecule and a protein. This method is often used in the search for new drug candidates throughout the drug research and development process. The docking process may be divided into three main parts: firstly, identifying a target protein that is usually linked to disease and is known to bind small molecules. Next, assembling a possible small molecules library that binds to a protein and may interfere with protein function. Finally, to find the optimal binding position and energy, every small molecule is then docked into the protein. The system must be carefully selected and prepared before doing any calculations.

Numerous crucial biological activities, such as DNA replication, RNA transcription, RNA splicing, nucleic acid breakdown, and protein synthesis, depend on interactions between proteins and nucleic acids. The interactions are implicated in a number of diseases, such as neurological disorders and cancer, *etc*. Therefore, it is important to know the structural details and interactions of protein-nucleic acid complexes. The traditional experimental approach is based on high-resolution methods to analyze the complex structure of protein-nucleic acid, which is a tedious and difficult process. In order to understand protein-nucleic acid interactions, computational tools are used to complement the experimental methods. By building theoretical models of the complex structures at the atomic level, *in silico* docking of proteins with nucleic acids can provide enough details to establish a working hypothesis and guide subsequent experimental analysis to identify key amino acid or nucleotide residues.

Protein-protein interactions are crucial in various aspects of cellular functions, such as signal transduction, cell communication, metabolic control, and gene regulation. Knowing the structural details of a protein-protein interaction helps to understand the interaction mechanism and the function of the proteins involved. Currently, the crystal structure of protein complexes is more difficult to obtain. Therefore, it requires fast and robust computational methods to reliably predict the structure of the protein-protein complexes. Protein-protein docking is a computational tool for predicting protein-protein interactions. By using state-of-the-art docking software tools, **Creative Biolabs** can find the relative transformation and conformation of two proteins that result in a stable complex. Our platform searches all potential binding modes in the translational and rotational space between the two proteins using computational docking techniques, and then it scores each pose according to its energy.

![Schematic illustration of the docking problem where two unbound units A and B interact with each other to form a bound configuration.](static/img/2-4-5-1-AI-assisted-Molecular-Docking-2.jpg)Fig.2 Protein-protein Docking.2

enriched_points.point_title: Application of AI in Molecular Docking
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI enhances molecular docking by accurately predicting ligand-protein interactions, optimizing binding affinities, and streamlining candidate selection, thus improving the drug discovery process.
Advanced AI models, such as generative adversarial networks, improve predictive accuracy by learning from vast chemical datasets, enabling faster identification of viable drug candidates.

AI-driven molecular docking significantly reduces drug development timelines, accelerating the transition from initial discovery to clinical trials, thus potentially decreasing costs and increasing success rates.
